title: Arrays & Pointers 👯‍♂️
description: The secret connection between arrays and RAM.

slides.title: The Big Secret 🤫
slides.content:
Are you ready for your mind to be blown?

**An Array is basically just a Pointer in disguise.**

When you create `int scores[5]`, the name `scores` isn't actually holding 5 numbers. The name `scores` is literally just an invisible pointer physically pointing to the memory address of the **first** element: `&scores[0]`.

slides.title: Proving the Secret
slides.content: Look at these addresses! Printing the array name prints the exact same memory address as `&array[0]`.

quizData.question: If you just write the name of an array by itself (without brackets `[]`), what does the C compiler interpret it as?

options.text: The entire contents of the array.
A pointer to the memory address of the first element in the array.

quizData.explanation: The naked array name 'decays' into a flat pointer pointing at Index 0.

slides.title: Pointer Arithmetic 🧮
slides.content:
Because an array name is just a pointer, you can do math on it!

If `scores` points to index 0, then `(scores + 1)` physically moves the pointer 1 slot forward in memory to point at index 1.

Writing `*(scores + 1)` is exactly the same as writing `scores[1]`. Brackets `[]` are literally just a convenient syntax disguise invented to make pointer math easier to read!

quizData.question: The array brackets syntax `Array[2]` is functionally identical to taking the base pointer of the array, moving forward 2 slots, and dereferencing it: `*(Array + 2)`.
quizData.explanation: Yes! The square brackets `[]` are literally "syntactic sugar" (a shortcut) for performing pointer arithmetic under the hood.
